cards.name: Airway compromise (anaplastic carcinoma)
cards.detail: Airway management is a paramount concern in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma. Tracheostomy is often a morbid and temporary treatment that may not be the option a patient would choose. Avoid prophylactic tracheostomy. Discuss palliative care options including airway management and IV steroids at the outset of diagnosis. Goals of therapy must be established early.

cards.name: Diarrhea and flushing (medullary carcinoma)
cards.detail: Many patients with advanced MTC have diarrhea and flushing because the tumor can secrete calcitonin and sometimes other hormonally active peptides. Treatment with somatostatin analogs (eg, octreotide, lanreotide) may be useful in patients with these symptoms.

cards.name: Symptoms of upper aerodigestive tract compression or invasion (medullary carcinoma)
cards.detail: Symptoms reported by up to 15% of patients with sporadic MTC. Surgical resection is the main treatment for MTC. In the presence of grossly invasive disease, more extended procedures with resection of involved neck structures may be appropriate.

cards.name: Immune checkpoint inhibitor-related toxicities
cards.detail: Immune checkpoint inhibitor-related toxicities require specific management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities

cards.name: Kinase inhibitor side effects (bleeding, hypertension, stroke, liver toxicity)
cards.detail: Kinase inhibitors may cause severe or fatal side effects including bleeding, hypertension, stroke, and liver toxicity

cards.name: Lenvatinib treatment-related deaths
cards.detail: Lenvatinib: six treatment-related deaths occurred in the lenvatinib group in the SELECT trial

cards.name: Sorafenib treatment-related death
cards.detail: Sorafenib: one treatment-related death occurred in the sorafenib group; hand-foot syndrome common and may require dose adjustments

cards.name: Hand-foot syndrome
cards.detail: Sorafenib: one treatment-related death occurred in the sorafenib group; hand-foot syndrome common and may require dose adjustments

cards.name: QTc interval prolongation
cards.detail: Vandetanib: cardiac toxicity involving prolongation of the QTc interval; restricted distribution through REMS program

cards.name: Severe bleeding (cabozantinib)
cards.detail: Cabozantinib: rare adverse events include severe bleeding and gastrointestinal perforations or fistulas; severe hemorrhage is a contraindication

cards.name: Gastrointestinal perforation/fistula
cards.detail: Cabozantinib: rare adverse events include severe bleeding and gastrointestinal perforations or fistulas; severe hemorrhage is a contraindication

cards.name: Fatigue (dabrafenib/trametinib)
cards.detail: Dabrafenib/trametinib: common adverse events include fatigue (38%), pyrexia (37%), and nausea (35%)

cards.name: Pyrexia (dabrafenib/trametinib)
cards.detail: Dabrafenib/trametinib: common adverse events include fatigue (38%), pyrexia (37%), and nausea (35%)

cards.name: Nausea (dabrafenib/trametinib)
cards.detail: Dabrafenib/trametinib: common adverse events include fatigue (38%), pyrexia (37%), and nausea (35%)

cards.name: Hypoparathyroidism
cards.detail: Hypoparathyroidism: most common significant complication of thyroidectomy; transient clinical hypoparathyroidism is common in adults and children undergoing total thyroidectomy; reported hypocalcemia in 5.4% immediately after total thyroidectomy, persisting in only 0.5% at 1 year

cards.name: Permanent hypocalcemia
cards.detail: Permanent hypocalcemia: reported incidence of 1.1%

cards.name: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury
cards.detail: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury: reported incidence of 3.4% for long-term injury

cards.name: Superior laryngeal nerve injury
cards.detail: Superior laryngeal nerve injury: under-reported and negatively impacts voice projection and high pitch range

cards.name: Salivary gland dysfunction
cards.detail: Radioactive iodine therapy risks: salivary gland dysfunction, lacrimal gland dysfunction, transient gonadal dysfunction, secondary primary malignancies (including leukemia and salivary gland malignancies in low-risk thyroid cancer patients receiving adjuvant RAI)

cards.name: Lacrimal gland dysfunction
cards.detail: Radioactive iodine therapy risks: salivary gland dysfunction, lacrimal gland dysfunction, transient gonadal dysfunction, secondary primary malignancies (including leukemia and salivary gland malignancies in low-risk thyroid cancer patients receiving adjuvant RAI)

cards.name: Gonadal dysfunction
cards.detail: Radioactive iodine therapy risks: salivary gland dysfunction, lacrimal gland dysfunction, transient gonadal dysfunction, secondary primary malignancies (including leukemia and salivary gland malignancies in low-risk thyroid cancer patients receiving adjuvant RAI)

cards.name: Secondary primary malignancy
cards.detail: Radioactive iodine therapy risks: salivary gland dysfunction, lacrimal gland dysfunction, transient gonadal dysfunction, secondary primary malignancies (including leukemia and salivary gland malignancies in low-risk thyroid cancer patients receiving adjuvant RAI)

cards.name: Nasolacrimal duct stenosis
cards.detail: Nasolacrimal duct stenosis may occur with RAI

cards.name: Pulmonary fibrosis (from RAI)
cards.detail: Diffuse lung metastases: maximum 48-hour whole-body dose should not exceed approximately 80 mCi to avoid pulmonary fibrosis

cards.name: Myelosuppression (from high cumulative RAI)
cards.detail: Patients with high (>1000 mCi) cumulative lifetime administered activities should be monitored for myelosuppression and potential long-term

labels: Symptoms of upper aerodigestive tract compression or invasion (medullary carcinoma)
